ai.0: Unit 4 Area of Study 1 is the story of three superb models — light as a wave, matter as particles, space and time as fixed — and the experiments that broke each of them. Read it in one sitting. Nothing here needs to be solved, only seen.
ai.1: Hold one question in mind for the whole of this page: what, exactly, is light? Newton said it was a stream of tiny particles — he called them corpuscles. His contemporary Huygens said it was a wave. The argument ran for more than a century, because both models explained the everyday evidence of reflection, refraction and shadows.
ai.2: The stakes are high: almost everything we know about the universe — the temperature of stars, the composition of galaxies, the structure of a virus — arrives as light. Get light wrong and you get everything wrong.
ai.3: This Area of Study — its official question is How has understanding about the physical world changed? — is built like a detective story. Each act opens with a confident model, introduces one stubborn experiment, and watches the model break. Figure 1.1 is the evidence board: stripes that particles cannot make, electrons ejected in a way waves cannot explain, and particles of matter drawing wave patterns. By the end, even space and time will have given way.
ai.4: Connects The Fields story ended with a promise: a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can regenerate each other, detach from every wire and charge, and travel through empty space — reread Where the field idea is heading. This page is that promise kept, and then broken, twice.
ai.5: In 1801 Thomas Young settled the old argument — apparently. He let light pass through two narrow slits onto a screen. If light were Newton's corpuscles, two bright stripes should appear, one behind each slit. Instead the screen showed many: a long ladder of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
ai.6: Only a wave can do this. Where two ripples on a pond meet crest on crest, they reinforce; crest on trough, they cancel. A bright band marks a place where the waves from the two slits arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ — and a dark band marks a half-wavelength of mismatch, (n + ½)λ, exactly as overlapping sound and water waves behave. The pattern is so lawful that one formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, connects the fringe spacing to the wavelength — Young had measured the wavelength of light with little more than a ruler.
ai.7: But a wave of what? The answer came in the 1860s from territory you already own. James Clerk Maxwell showed that a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and that this self-sustaining pair must travel — at a speed his equations fixed at 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹, exactly the measured speed of light. Light, then, can be modelled as a transverse electromagnetic wave, produced wherever charges accelerate, and every electromagnetic wave — radio to gamma — travels at the same speed c in a vacuum.
ai.8: You have already met the production line. Electrons forced around the Australian Synchrotron's storage ring are continuously accelerating — circular motion is acceleration — and accelerating charges radiate. That is why the machine glows. By 1900, with Young's stripes and Maxwell's fields in agreement, the case looked closed.
ai.9: Connects The wave model is built properly in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference. The fields doing the waving are the ones you mapped in Unit 3.
ai.10: The experiment that reopened the case looks harmless. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface and electrons jump out of it — the photoelectric effect.
ai.11: The wave model makes three confident predictions about it. Brighter light carries more energy, so the ejected electrons should leave faster. Any colour should work, given time for the metal to soak up energy. And in dim light there should be a delay while the energy accumulates.
ai.12: Experiment said no, three times. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons depends only on the light's frequency — turning up the brightness ejects more electrons, never faster ones. Below a threshold frequency, no electrons leave at all, at any brightness. And emission is effectively instantaneous, even in the dimmest light.
ai.13: In 1905 Einstein proposed something the wave model could not survive: light delivers its energy in indivisible lumps. Each lump — each photon — carries an energy named by E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck's constant, an absurdly small 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which pays the metal's exit fee — the work function φ — and keeps the change as kinetic energy: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet. Every awkward observation follows at a stroke: frequency sets the energy of each lump, brightness only counts the lumps, and a lump arrives whole or not at all.
ai.14: The measuring instrument is pure Unit 3. To find the electrons' energy, experimenters apply a reverse voltage and increase it until even the fastest electron no longer crosses — the stopping voltage — which is W = qV not on sheet run backwards, as a brake instead of an accelerator. And the electron-volt, the energy unit born when you accelerated charges through potential differences, becomes the working currency of everything that follows.
ai.15: Connects The photon and the full photoelectric analysis live in chapter 14 · The photon; the stopping-voltage idea and the electron-volt were banked in Electric fields · Work and energy.
ai.16: In 1924 a French physicist, Louis de Broglie, asked the symmetric question. If light — supposedly a wave — behaves as a stream of particles, might matter — supposedly particles — behave as a wave? He proposed that every moving object carries a wavelength, named by λ = h/p on formula sheet: the larger the momentum, the shorter the wave.
ai.17: Three years later the test was run: fire a beam of electrons at a thin crystal. The beam emerged drawing concentric rings on a screen — the same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through the same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. The electron, the particle you accelerated with voltages and steered into circles with magnetic fields in Unit 3, diffracts.
ai.18: Why does a cricket ball not diffract through a doorway? Because h is so small. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength of about 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m — unimaginably smaller than any gap it could ever pass through, and diffraction only becomes noticeable when the wavelength is comparable to the opening. Electrons are so light that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms, which is why crystals make perfect diffraction gratings for them.
ai.19: The deepest version of the experiment is the one Simulation 1.1 lets you run. Send electrons — or photons — through Young's double slits one at a time. Each arrives at the screen as a single localised dot: a particle. But as thousands of dots accumulate, the interference fringes assemble themselves out of the statistics: a wave — no single particle makes a pattern, yet every particle obeys one.
ai.20: Connects Matter waves get their own chapter at chapter 15 · Matter waves. The electron beam that stars in it is the one you built in Motors & accelerators — same electrons, new identity.
ai.21: Two awkward facts about atoms were waiting for these ideas. First, atoms should not exist: a classical electron circling a nucleus is accelerating, and accelerating charges radiate — Maxwell's own rule, the one that lights the synchrotron — so the electron should broadcast its energy away and spiral into the nucleus in a fraction of a second.
ai.22: Second, glowing gases do not emit a rainbow. Each element emits only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, a barcode unique to that element. Sodium street lamps glow with their twin yellow lines; metal vapour lamps and neon signs each show their own barcode; sunlight carries thousands of dark lines where cooler gas has subtracted exactly those colours.
ai.23: The explanation is quantisation. An electron bound in an atom can hold only certain energies — rungs on a ladder, with nothing in between. Drop from a higher rung to a lower one and the atom emits a single photon whose energy E = hf exactly matches the gap; absorb a photon of exactly a gap's energy and the electron climbs. Bright emission lines and dark absorption lines are the same ladder read in opposite directions, and reading a spectrum becomes reading the atom's energy levels.
ai.24: But why rungs at all? De Broglie's wave answers this too. Wrap the electron's wave around the atom and it must close on itself smoothly — a standing wave, like a guitar string that can play only its harmonics. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop, so only certain energies are allowed.
ai.25: The picture is a simplified model, refined by later physics, but it explains the one thing the classical atom never could: why an atom has a lowest rung — a ground state — below which the electron cannot fall, and therefore why atoms are stable. Quantisation is not a quirk of light; it is the architecture of matter.
ai.26: Connects Spectra and energy levels are read properly in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; the standing waves that make the rungs are rehearsed with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
ai.27: One loose thread remained from the wave's first victory. Every wave known to physics waves in something — so what carries light across empty space? Physicists named the proposed medium the aether, and in 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument exquisite enough to find it: two light beams raced along perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, the whole apparatus turned and re-tried through the seasons as Earth swung around the Sun. If Earth moves through an aether, the beam times should differ — they never did, the most famous null result in physics.
ai.28: In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein took the result at face value and rebuilt mechanics from two postulates: the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame of reference, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, regardless of the motion of the source or the observer. The second postulate sounds innocent. It is not. If everyone measures the same c, then time and distance themselves must differ between observers.
ai.29: The consequences carry names you will use all year. Moving clocks run slow — time dilation, named by t = γt0 on formula sheet. Moving lengths shrink along the direction of travel — length contraction, named by L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
ai.30: The factor γ, the Lorentz factor, is barely above 1 at everyday speeds — which is why nobody noticed for three centuries — and grows without limit as speed approaches c. The fine print "v << c" attached to Unit 3's circular-path formula was this warning, planted a unit early.
ai.31: The evidence falls out of the sky. Muons — heavy, short-lived cousins of the electron, created kilometres overhead by cosmic rays — live about 2.2 μs on average. Even at 0.995c that allows roughly 660 m of travel, yet they arrive at the ground in abundance. In the ground frame of reference their internal clocks run about ten times slow, so they live long enough to make the trip; in the muon's frame of reference the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold, so the trip is short enough to survive — two descriptions, one agreed outcome.
ai.32: And the satellites that locate your phone carry clocks that tick slow by a few microseconds each day because of their orbital speed — left uncorrected, GPS fixes would drift by kilometres every day. Your phone applies special relativity every time it finds you.
ai.33: Connects Postulates, proper time and the muon story are built in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy. The "v << c" fine print was planted on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
ai.34: Einstein's relativity papers of 1905 ended with a short afterthought that outgrew everything else: mass is a concentrated form of energy. The rest energy locked in any object is named by E0 = mc² on formula sheet, and because the exchange rate is c², a teaspoon of water holds enough rest energy to supply twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year. Set the object moving and its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the kinetic share named by Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet — at everyday speeds this collapses back to the familiar formula, one more approximation with its fine print now visible.
ai.35: The Sun runs on this exchange. In its core, hydrogen fuses into helium, and the products weigh slightly less than the ingredients; the missing mass leaves as light. The Sun converts about 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of itself into radiated energy every second — sunshine is the Sun's mass, spent at the universal rate.
ai.36: The exchange runs in both directions. When an electron meets its antiparticle, the positron, both vanish and two gamma-ray photons fly apart in opposite directions — opposite, because momentum must still balance. Hospital PET scanners image the body by catching exactly those photon pairs.
ai.37: And in particle accelerators the trade reverses: the kinetic energy of a collision condenses into new particles that did not exist before the impact. The synchrotron you studied as a circular-motion exercise is, in the fine print, a machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.
ai.38: Connects The full energy ledger is settled in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that spend it were built in Motors & accelerators.
ai.39: You have now read the whole Area of Study once: a wave that turned out to arrive in lumps, particles that turned out to interfere, atoms saved by standing waves, and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass as concentrated energy. Everything from here is the same story at higher resolution.
ai.40: Pass 2 carries it in six chapters, numbered 12 to 17, each meant to be read the night before class meets the topic: light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy. There you will find every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and quick checks to confirm each idea landed.
ai.41: Pass 3 is for exam season. Four pages walk the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. The depth row below carries you down whenever you are ready, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts to obscure the story.
ai.42: Connects Begin Pass 2 · chapter 12 · Light as a wave whenever you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Light as a wave for when the exam is real.

socratic.0:
Socrates. Let us begin together, you and I, with three convictions long held to be settled: that light is a wave, that matter is made of particles, and that space and time are fixed for all. Tell me — would it trouble you to learn that experiment overturned every one of them?
Student. It would surprise me, certainly. Are we to prove this here?
Socrates. Not to prove — we have nothing to solve. We need only walk over the whole terrain once, as one walks a field before working it, and see how each conviction fell.
socratic.1:
Socrates. So tell me first: what is light?
Student. I should have to think. Some have said it is a stream of tiny particles.
Socrates. Just so — Newton said exactly that, and called them corpuscles. But his contemporary Huygens insisted it was a wave. Now, which of them was right?
Student. Surely one could simply test it and see.
Socrates. One would think so. Yet the dispute ran more than a century. Why, do you suppose, was it so hard to settle?
Student. Perhaps both pictures explained the same things?
Socrates. Precisely. Both accounted for reflection, refraction, and the casting of shadows. The everyday evidence could not choose between them.
socratic.2:
Socrates. And does it much matter which picture we hold? It is, after all, only light.
Student. I want to say it matters little — but I suspect you are leading me somewhere.
Socrates. Then consider: how do you know the temperature of a star? The substances in a distant galaxy? The very shape of a virus?
Student. All of those reach us as light, do they not.
Socrates. Almost all that we know of the universe arrives so. And if our account of light is wrong —?
Student. Then our account of everything resting upon it is wrong too. I see it is not a small matter at all.
socratic.3:
Socrates. The official question we are set is this — How has understanding about the physical world changed? — and we shall answer it in stages. Can you guess the shape each stage will take?
Student. Begin with the accepted model, I would think.
Socrates. Yes — open with a settled model, set against it one stubborn experimental result, and follow the model until it breaks. Figure 1.1 lays out the evidence we shall meet: stripes no particle could make, electrons flung off in a way no wave can explain, and particles of matter that draw wave patterns. And by the end —?
Student. You hinted already. Space and time themselves are revised.
Socrates. They are.
socratic.4:
Connects Socrates. Cast your mind back to where the Fields material left us. What was its parting result?
Student. That a changing electric field and a changing magnetic field can regenerate each other, break free of every wire and charge, and travel through empty space — it is set out in Where the field idea is heading.
Socrates. That very travelling thing is what this page takes up. And then watches overturned — twice over.
socratic.5:
Socrates. Now to the experiment many thought decisive. In 1801 Thomas Young sent light through two narrow slits onto a screen. If Newton were right, and light were corpuscles, what should appear on that screen?
Student. Two bright stripes, I should think — one behind each slit, where the particles land.
Socrates. A reasonable expectation. But that is not what Young saw. He found not two stripes but many — a long row of evenly spaced bright and dark bands.
Student. Many bands? From only two slits? Particles could never do that.
socratic.6:
Socrates. They could not. What, then, can?
Student. A wave, surely — like ripples meeting on a pond.
Socrates. Tell me what ripples do when they meet.
Student. Crest on crest, they reinforce; crest on trough, they cancel.
Socrates. So a bright band marks —?
Student. Where the two waves arrive in step — a path difference of a whole number of wavelengths, nλ.
Socrates. And a dark band?
Student. A half-wavelength of mismatch — (n + ½)λ — just as overlapping sound and water waves behave.
Socrates. And the pattern is so regular that a single formula, Δx = λL/d on formula sheet, ties the fringe spacing to the wavelength. So with little more than a ruler, what had Young in fact measured?
Student. The wavelength of light itself.
socratic.7:
Socrates. If light is a wave, one ought to ask: what is waving?
Student. For ripples it is water. For light I have no answer.
Socrates. Nor had anyone, until the 1860s, when Maxwell built the answer from what was already known. He showed that a changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which remakes the electric field — and that this self-sustaining pair must travel. At what speed, do you suppose, did his equations fix it?
Student. I cannot guess the number.
Socrates. 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ — the measured speed of light, exactly. What would you conclude?
Student. That light is that very pair — a transverse electromagnetic wave.
Socrates. Produced wherever charges accelerate. And every such wave, from radio to gamma, travels at the one speed c in a vacuum.
socratic.8:
Socrates. You have met this production already, though you may not recall where. Think of the Australian Synchrotron — electrons forced round its storage ring. Are they accelerating?
Student. Circular motion is acceleration, yes — even at constant speed the direction turns.
Socrates. And accelerating charges?
Student. Radiate. So that is why the machine pours out light.
Socrates. Just so. By 1900, with Young's pattern and Maxwell's fields in agreement, would you have called the matter closed?
Student. I would have. Light is a wave, and there's an end of it.
socratic.9:
Connects Socrates. Where might you pursue this wave picture in its full depth?
Student. The wave model in chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and the interference evidence in chapter 13 · Diffraction & interference.
Socrates. And the fields beneath it all are those you mapped in Unit 3.
socratic.10:
Socrates. But you spoke too soon in closing the matter. A second experiment reopened it — and it looks utterly plain. Shine ultraviolet light on a clean metal surface. What happens?
Student. Something is given off?
Socrates. Electrons are ejected from the metal. This is the photoelectric effect. Plain enough — yet within it the wave model meets its ruin.
socratic.11:
Socrates. Let us hold to the wave model and reason out what it predicts. First: brighter light — what does it carry?
Student. More energy. So the electrons should come off faster.
Socrates. Second: must the light be of any particular colour?
Student. I see no reason. Any colour should serve, if the metal is given time to soak up enough energy.
Socrates. And third, in very dim light?
Student. There should be a delay — a pause while the energy gathers.
socratic.12:
Socrates. Three clean predictions. Now hear what experiment reported. The greatest kinetic energy of the electrons depends on one thing only — the light's frequency. Brightness changes nothing of their speed; it only ejects more of them. What of your first prediction?
Student. Overturned. Brighter light should have meant faster, and it did not.
Socrates. Below a certain threshold frequency, no electron emerges at all — however blinding the brightness. Your second?
Student. Also wrong. Some colours simply will not do, at any intensity.
Socrates. And emission is effectively instant, even in the faintest light. Your third?
Student. Gone as well. There is no delay. The wave model has failed on all three counts.
socratic.13:
Socrates. So in 1905 Einstein offered an account no wave could supply: light delivers its energy in indivisible amounts. Each amount — each photon — carries an energy E = hf on formula sheet, where h is Planck's constant, 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. Now reason with me. One photon gives all its energy to one electron, which must first pay the metal's exit price — the work function φ — and keeps what remains. So the electron's energy is —?
Student. The photon's energy less the toll: Ek max = hf − φ on formula sheet.
Socrates. Now see if your three puzzles dissolve. Why does frequency alone set the energy?
Student. Because E = hf fixes each amount. Brightness only sends more amounts — more electrons, not faster ones. And an amount arrives whole or not at all, so a too-feeble photon does nothing, and there is no waiting. Every observation follows.
socratic.14:
Socrates. One question of method. How would you measure how fast the fastest electron flies?
Student. I am not sure one can catch it directly.
Socrates. Then turn a tool from Unit 3 against it. Apply a reverse voltage and raise it until even the swiftest electron can no longer cross. What have you found at that point?
Student. The stopping voltage — it is W = qV not on sheet, but run backwards, a brake rather than an accelerator.
Socrates. And the energy unit born when charges were accelerated through potential differences — the electron-volt — becomes from here onward the working currency of all that follows.
socratic.15:
Connects Socrates. Where is the photon's full reckoning laid out?
Student. The photon and the whole photoelectric analysis in chapter 14 · The photon; and the stopping voltage and the electron-volt were first set down in Electric fields · Work and energy.
Socrates. Good. Keep both within reach.
socratic.16:
Socrates. Now turn the question on its head. We have seen light, called a wave, behave as a stream of particles. In 1924 de Broglie asked the mirror question. Can you frame it yourself?
Student. If a wave can act as particles, then perhaps matter — which we call particles — can act as a wave.
Socrates. Exactly his thought. He proposed that every moving thing carries a wavelength, λ = h/p on formula sheet. And as the momentum grows?
Student. The wave shortens. So the heavier and faster a thing, the smaller its wave.
socratic.17:
Socrates. Three years on, the wager was tested: a beam of electrons fired at a thin crystal. What do you imagine emerged on the screen?
Student. If de Broglie is right — a wave pattern?
Socrates. Concentric rings — the very same family of pattern that X-rays of equal wavelength make through that same crystal, as panel (c) of Figure 1.1 shows. So tell me what the electron has done.
Student. The electron — the same particle accelerated by voltages and steered into circles by magnetic fields in Unit 3 — has diffracted. Matter waves.
socratic.18:
Socrates. Then why does a cricket ball not diffract as it passes through a doorway?
Student. I suppose its wave is too small to notice — because h is so very small.
Socrates. A 150 g ball bowled at 20 m s⁻¹ has a de Broglie wavelength near 2.2 × 10⁻³⁴ m. Compare that to any doorway.
Student. Vanishingly smaller. And diffraction shows only when the wavelength is near the size of the opening.
Socrates. So why do electrons oblige where cricket balls will not?
Student. They are so light that their wavelengths match the spacing between atoms — which is why a crystal works as a diffraction grating for them.
socratic.19:
Socrates. Now the deepest form of the experiment, the one Simulation 1.1 shows. Send electrons — or photons — through Young's two slits, but one at a time. How does each one arrive?
Student. As a single dot, I would expect — a particle striking one spot.
Socrates. A single localised dot, yes. Now let thousands gather. What forms?
Student. Surely just a smudge behind the slits?
Socrates. The interference fringes form — out of the statistics alone. No single particle makes a pattern, yet every particle obeys one. Does that not sit strangely with you?
Student. It does. Each is a particle, and all together are a wave. I am not sure I can picture how both are true.
socratic.20:
Connects Socrates. Where is this strangeness given its full treatment?
Student. Matter waves in chapter 15 · Matter waves — and the electron beam there is the one built in Motors & accelerators.
Socrates. The same electrons, cast in a new role.
socratic.21:
Socrates. Let us bring these new ideas to bear on the atom, for two old wounds were waiting there. The first concerns stability. On the classical account, picture an electron circling a nucleus. Is it accelerating?
Student. Yes — it is forever turning, so yes.
Socrates. And an accelerating charge, by Maxwell's own rule — the one that lights the synchrotron —?
Student. Radiates. So the electron should bleed away its energy and spiral into the nucleus.
Socrates. In how long, do you think?
Student. If the rule holds, a mere fraction of a second. Yet atoms plainly endure. That should not be possible.
socratic.22:
Socrates. The second wound concerns the light a glowing gas gives off. Would you expect a smooth spread of every colour?
Student. I would have, like a rainbow.
Socrates. Yet each element emits only certain sharp colours — a line spectrum, unique to it like a signature. Sodium street lamps show their two yellow lines; metal-vapour lamps and neon signs each their own; and sunlight is crossed by thousands of dark lines, where cooler gas has stolen exactly those colours.
Student. So the colours are picked out, not poured out. Why only those, and no others?
socratic.23:
Socrates. The answer is quantisation. Suppose an electron bound in an atom may hold only certain energies — discrete levels, with nothing between. Now, what must happen when it drops from a higher level to a lower?
Student. It must shed the difference. As a photon, I expect.
Socrates. A single photon whose energy E = hf matches the gap exactly. And to climb?
Student. It must absorb a photon of precisely that gap's energy.
Socrates. So the bright emission lines and the dark absorption lines —?
Student. Are the same set of levels read in opposite directions. The spectrum lays bare the atom's energy levels.
socratic.24:
Socrates. And here de Broglie's wave returns to earn its keep. Why should the levels be discrete at all? Picture the electron's wave wrapped round the atom. What must it do to survive?
Student. Close on itself smoothly, I suppose — meet its own tail.
Socrates. A standing wave, like a string that sounds only its harmonics. And so?
Student. Only certain wavelengths fit the loop — and therefore only certain energies are allowed. That is why the levels are discrete.
socratic.25:
Socrates. This is a simplified picture, mind — later physics refines it. But it earns the one thing the classical atom could never explain. Can you name it?
Student. Why an atom has a lowest level — a ground state — beneath which the electron cannot fall.
Socrates. And from that, what follows?
Student. That atoms are stable. The electron simply has no lower step to spiral down to.
Socrates. So you see quantisation is not light's affair alone.
Student. No — it runs through matter itself.
socratic.26:
Connects Socrates. And where shall you find the spectra worked out fully?
Student. Spectra and energy levels in chapter 16 · Atoms and spectra; and the standing waves that make the levels are introduced with light itself in chapter 12 · Light as a wave.
socratic.27:
Socrates. One loose thread still trails from the wave model's first triumph. Every wave you know waves in something. So light, crossing empty space, waves in —?
Student. I cannot say. There is nothing out there to wave.
Socrates. The physicists supposed there must be, and named it the aether. In 1887 Michelson and Morley built an instrument fine enough to catch it: two light beams ran down perpendicular arms, recombined to interfere, and the whole was turned and tested again through the seasons as Earth swept round the Sun. If Earth moves through an aether, what should they have found?
Student. The beam times should differ.
Socrates. They never did — not once. The most famous null result in all of physics.
socratic.28:
Socrates. In 1905 — the same year as the photon — Einstein did not explain the null result away. He took it as true and rebuilt mechanics on two postulates. First, the laws of physics are the same in every inertial frame. Second, the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for every observer, whatever the motion of source or observer. Does that second one trouble you?
Student. It sounds harmless enough.
Socrates. Then chase it. If every observer, however he moves, measures the very same c — what must give way?
Student. If the speed cannot change between them... then the time and the distance must. Time and length themselves differ from observer to observer.
socratic.29:
Socrates. And these consequences carry names you will use all year. A clock in motion — what does it do?
Student. Runs slow. Time dilation — t = γt0 on formula sheet.
Socrates. And a length in motion?
Student. Shrinks along the direction it travels. Length contraction — L = L0/γ on formula sheet.
socratic.30:
Socrates. That factor γ — the Lorentz factor — how large is it at the speeds of ordinary life?
Student. Only a hair above 1, I would guess, since no one noticed these effects for centuries.
Socrates. Just barely above 1. And as speed climbs toward c?
Student. It grows without limit.
Socrates. Recall the qualification "v << c" pinned to Unit 3's circular-path formula. What was it quietly warning you of?
Student. Of exactly this — a unit before I could see why.
socratic.31:
Socrates. You may doubt all this. So where can we see it in nature? Consider the muon — a heavy, short-lived cousin of the electron, made kilometres overhead by cosmic rays. It lives, on average, about 2.2 μs. Even at 0.995c, how far should it get?
Student. Roughly 660 m, by that lifetime. So it should never reach the ground.
Socrates. And yet muons arrive at the surface in great numbers. How, in the ground's frame?
Student. Their internal clocks run about ten times slow — so they live long enough to finish the trip.
Socrates. And in the muon's own frame, where its clock is normal?
Student. Then the atmosphere is contracted about tenfold — so the journey is short enough to finish. Two descriptions, one outcome.
socratic.32:
Socrates. You think this remote from your life. Yet how does your phone know where you stand?
Student. By the satellites overhead.
Socrates. Whose clocks, owing to their orbital speed, run slow by a few microseconds each day. Leave that uncorrected, and what becomes of your position?
Student. It would drift by kilometres a day. So my phone is quietly applying special relativity every time it finds me.
Socrates. Every single time.
socratic.33:
Connects Socrates. And where is all this set out in full?
Student. The postulates, proper time, and the muon evidence in chapter 17 · Relativity & mass–energy — and that "v << c" qualification was first met on the circular paths of Magnetic fields · Circular paths.
socratic.34:
Socrates. Einstein's 1905 papers ended on a brief result that outweighed all the rest: mass is a concentrated form of energy. The rest energy in any object is E0 = mc² on formula sheet. Now, the conversion factor is c² — an enormous number. What does that imply for a mere teaspoon of water?
Student. If c² multiplies it... a great deal of energy.
Socrates. Enough to supply twenty thousand homes with electricity for a year. And set the object moving — its total energy becomes γmc² on formula sheet, with the moving share Ek = (γ − 1)mc² on formula sheet. And at everyday speeds?
Student. It collapses back to the familiar kinetic-energy formula — another approximation whose small print I now understand.
socratic.35:
Socrates. The Sun lives by this very conversion. In its core hydrogen fuses into helium — and the products weigh slightly less than the reactants. Where has the missing mass gone?
Student. Emitted as light, I should think — turned into energy.
Socrates. About 4.2 × 10⁹ kg of the Sun's own substance, converted to radiated energy each second. So when sunlight warms your face —?
Student. I am feeling the Sun's mass itself, converted at the universal rate. That is a strange thought.
socratic.36:
Socrates. And the conversion runs both ways. Let an electron meet its antiparticle, the positron. What becomes of them?
Student. Both are annihilated — turned to energy.
Socrates. Into two gamma-ray photons, flying apart in opposite directions. Why opposite, do you suppose?
Student. Because momentum must still balance — they cannot both go the same way.
Socrates. Just so. And hospital PET scanners image the body by catching exactly these photon pairs.
socratic.37:
Socrates. In a particle accelerator the conversion goes the other way still. The kinetic energy of a collision becomes —?
Student. New particles? Ones that did not exist before the impact?
Socrates. Created out of motion's energy. So the synchrotron you once studied as a tidy circular-motion exercise is, more truly, what?
Student. A machine whose every magnet is engineered around γ.
socratic.38:
Connects Socrates. And where is this whole energy account developed?
Student. The full account in chapter 17 · Mass–energy, and the accelerators that supply the energy in Motors & accelerators.
socratic.39:
Socrates. We have now walked the whole field once. Recount for me what we have seen.
Student. A wave that proved to arrive in discrete amounts; particles that proved to interfere; atoms held together by standing waves; and a speed limit that bends time and length and reveals mass as concentrated energy.
Socrates. Every settled conviction overturned, as I promised. And what comes next?
Student. The same ground again, I take it — only deeper.
Socrates. The same material, at greater depth.
socratic.40:
Socrates. Pass 2 takes it up in six chapters, numbered 12 to 17 — each meant to be read the night before its class. Can you name their march?
Student. Light as a wave; diffraction and interference; the photon; matter waves; atoms and spectra; relativity and mass–energy.
Socrates. And each carries every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard graphs, and checks to confirm the idea has truly landed.
socratic.41:
Socrates. Pass 3 is for the examination itself. Four pages cover the twenty-eight key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — light as a wave; light and matter as particles; light–matter similarities; relativity and mass–energy — with the subtleties, the common errors, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions answered in full. And should the detail ever cloud the whole?
Student. The depth row leads downward when I want it — and this page stays one click away whenever I need to see the shape of it all again.
socratic.42:
Connects Socrates. Then begin your descent where you will.
Student. Pass 2 opens at Pass 2 · chapter 12 · Light as a wave, and I shall bookmark Pass 3 · Light as a wave for when the exam draws near.
Socrates. Go well, then — and keep asking.
